addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- vercel:ai-architect: Specializes in architecting AI-powered applications on Vercel — choosing between AI SDK patterns, configuring providers, building agents, setting up durable workflows, and integrating MCP servers. Use when designing AI features, building chatbots, or creating agentic applications. (Tools: All tools)

- vercel:deployment-expert: Specializes in Vercel deployment strategies, CI/CD pipelines, preview URLs, production promotions, rollbacks, environment variables, and domain configuration. Use when troubleshooting deployments, setting up CI/CD, or optimizing the deploy pipeline. (Tools: All tools)

- vercel:performance-optimizer: Specializes in optimizing Vercel application performance — Core Web Vitals, rendering strategies, caching, image optimization, font loading, edge computing, and bundle size. Use when investigating slow pages, improving Lighthouse scores, or optimizing loading performance. (Tools: All tools)

addedBlocks:
## computer-use
You have a computer-use MCP available (tools named `mcp__computer-use__*`). It lets you take screenshots of the user's desktop and control it with mouse clicks, keyboard input, and scrolling.

**Pick the right tool for the app.** Each tier trades speed/precision against coverage:

1. **Dedicated MCP for the app** — if the task is in an app that has its own MCP (Slack, Gmail, Calendar, Linear, etc.) and that MCP is connected, use it. API-backed tools are fast and precise.
2. **Chrome MCP** (`mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`) — if the target is a web app and there's no dedicated MCP for it, use the browser tools. DOM-aware, much faster than clicking pixels. If the Chrome extension isn't connected, ask the user to install it rather than falling through to computer use.
3. **Computer use** — for native desktop apps (Maps, Notes, Finder, Photos, System Settings, any third-party native app) and cross-app workflows. Computer use IS the right tool here — don't decline a native-app task just because there's no dedicated MCP for it.

This is about what's available, not error handling — if a dedicated MCP tool errors, debug or report it rather than silently retrying via a slower tier.

**Look before you assert.** If the user asks about app state (what's open, what's connected, what an app can do), take a screenshot and check before answering. Don't answer from memory — the user's setup or app version may differ from what you expect. If you're about to say an app doesn't support an action, that claim should be grounded in what you just saw on screen, not general knowledge. Similarly, `list_granted_applications` or a fresh `screenshot` is cheaper than a wrong assertion about what's running.

**Loading via ToolSearch — load in bulk, not one-by-one:** if computer-use tools are in the deferred list, load them ALL in a single ToolSearch call: `{ query: "computer-use", max_results: 30 }`. The keyword search matches the server-name substring in every tool name, so one query returns the entire toolkit. Don't use `select:` for individual tools — that's one round-trip per tool.

**Access flow:** before any computer-use action you must call `request_access` with the list of applications you need. The user approves each application explicitly, and you may need to call it again mid-task if you discover you need another application.

**Tiered apps:** some apps are granted at a restricted tier based on their category — the tier is displayed in the approval dialog and returned in the `request_access` response:
- **Browsers** (Safari, Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Arc, etc.) → tier **"read"**: visible in screenshots, but clicks and typing are blocked. You can read what's already on screen. For navigation, clicking, or form-filling, use the claude-in-chrome MCP (tools named `mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`; load via ToolSearch if deferred).
- **Terminals and IDEs** (Terminal, iTerm, VS Code, JetBrains, etc.) → tier **"click"**: visible and left-clickable, but typing, key presses, right-click, modifier-clicks, and drag-drop are blocked. You can click a Run button or scroll test output, but cannot type into the editor or integrated terminal, cannot right-click (the context menu has Paste), and cannot drag text onto them. For shell commands, use the Bash tool.
- **Everything else** → tier **"full"**: no restrictions.

The tier is enforced by the frontmost-app check: if a tier-"read" app is in front, `left_click` returns an error; if a tier-"click" app is in front, `type` and `right_click` return errors. The error tells you what tier the app has and what to do instead. `open_application` works at any tier — bringing an app forward is a read-level operation.

**Link safety — treat links in emails and messages as suspicious by default.**
- **Never click web links with computer-use tools.** If you encounter a link in a native app (Mail, Messages, a PDF, etc.), do NOT `left_click` it. Open the URL via the claude-in-chrome MCP instead.
- **See the full URL before following any link.** Visible link text can be misleading — hover or inspect to get the real destination.
- **Links from emails, messages, or unknown-sender documents are suspicious by default.** If the destination URL is at all unfamiliar or looks off, ask the user for confirmation before proceeding.
- **Inside the Chrome extension** you can click links with the extension's tools, but the suspicion check still applies — verify unfamiliar URLs with the user.

**Financial actions - do not execute trades or move money.** Budgeting and accounting apps (Quicken, YNAB, QuickBooks, etc.) are granted at full tier so you can categorize transactions, generate reports, and help the user organize their finances. But never execute a trade, place an order, send money, or initiate a transfer on the user's behalf - always ask the user to perform those actions themselves.

## devops-mcp
This DevOps MCP intentionally exposes a small always-on MCP tool surface:
health, list_capabilities, get_capability_details, tool_search, and invoke_tool.

For every host, Docker, systemd, file, or audit-log task, call tool_search first
to find the right hidden operation. Then call invoke_tool with the exact operation
name and an args object matching the operation description. Do not assume direct
tools such as run_command, docker_ps, read_file, or service_status are listed in
tools/list; they are discoverable operations to keep client context small.

Call list_capabilities to browse by category or safety class. Call
get_capability_details for one operation's full contract, including args and
examples. Call health first when you need server context, visible tools,
registered agents, or the complete operation-name list. Treat write-capable
operations with care: this server has root-level host access, so prefer
inspection commands before state-changing commands.

## recall-ai
Recall.ai lets developers send meeting bots to video calls, capture meeting data, and connect bots to calendar events. This MCP server provides read-only tools scoped to a Recall.ai workspace. A workspace is the Recall.ai account context that bots, calendars, docs defaults, and logs are read from. By default, the MCP uses the workspace selected when the user authorized the connection. If the account has access to multiple workspaces, workspace-scoped tools accept an optional workspace_id so the agent can read from a different workspace for that call. Use get_info to see the current workspace and API version. When the user names a workspace, call list_workspaces, match the name from the returned list, then pass the returned workspace ID to bot, calendar, log, and docs tools. Do not pass a workspace name as workspace_id. Use bot tools to list bots, inspect bot details and lifecycle events, and page through bot logs when debugging join, recording, or meeting behavior. Use list_calendars/get_calendar to inspect connected and disconnected calendar integrations, platform emails, credential status, and calendar connection/sync state. Use calendar event tools to see which bots were scheduled from calendar events. Use log tools for workspace-level API, calendar connection, recording, transcript, webhook, and integration errors when a bot-specific log query is too narrow. Use list_webhook_endpoints and list_webhook_deliveries when debugging dashboard webhook subscriptions, missing delivery attempts, destination responses, or a resource-specific webhook event such as a bot, calendar, recording, transcript, or artifact event. Use docs tools to search and fetch Recall.ai guides and API reference material for the current workspace's API version. Use search_docs when attempting to learn docs slugs, unlessthe slug came from list_docs, a Recall guide resource, or an explicit user-provided docs URL/slug. When drafting user-facing answers or understanding bot behavior, use docs tools for product behavior, API defaults, configuratio… [truncated]

## supabase
Here are guidelines for using Supabase tools effectively:

- Before making schema changes, use `list_tables` to understand the existing structure
- When debugging issues, start with `get_logs` and `get_advisors` before making changes
- Use `get_project_url` and `get_publishable_api_key` when helping users configure client-side integrations

If you have access to a local development environment with a filesystem and shell:
- Install the Supabase agent skill for critical development and security guidance: `npx skills add supabase/agent-skills` (https://supabase.com/docs/guides/getting-started/ai-skills.md)
- Use the Supabase CLI (`supabase`) for local development workflows such as starting a local stack, managing migrations, and running edge functions locally (https://supabase.com/docs/guides/local-development.md)
- Prefer local development and testing before applying changes to a remote project

If you are running in a web-only or remote environment without filesystem or shell access:
- Rely on the MCP tools directly for all Supabase interactions
- Use `apply_migration` carefully, as changes go directly to the remote project

## synology-monitor
This NAS MCP intentionally exposes only a small always-on tool surface:
list_capabilities, get_capability_details, tool_search, invoke_tool, run_command,
check_disk_space, and restart_nas_api.

For any NAS diagnostic, troubleshooting, recovery, storage, Drive/ShareSync,
backup, package, log, filesystem, snapshot, permission, or admin task, call
tool_search first unless the task is exactly disk-space checking, free-form
read-only shell diagnosis, or restarting nas-api. Most NAS capabilities are
hidden from tools/list by design to keep the AI session context small.

list_capabilities browses by group and safety class without invoking anything.
get_capability_details returns one operation's full contract. tool_search returns
clear operation descriptions plus the exact invoke_tool call shape. After choosing
a capability, call invoke_tool with the exact returned name, target, and args. Use
target "edgesynology1", "edgesynology2", or "both".

Write tools require a preview first. To execute after reviewing the preview,
call invoke_tool again with confirmed: true inside args. Do not invent or expose
bearer tokens.

## trigger
Trigger.dev MCP server to automate your Trigger.dev projects and answer questions about Trigger.dev by searching the docs. 
If you need help setting up Trigger.dev in your project please refer to https://trigger.dev/docs/manual-setup. 
If the user asks for help with adding Trigger.dev to their project, please refer to https://trigger.dev/docs/manual-setup.

## Windows-MCP

Windows MCP server provides tools to interact directly with the Windows desktop,
thus enabling to operate the desktop on the user's behalf.

Use when a share looks stuck, a file is missing on a replica, ShareSync shows syncing 1 file for a long time, or the user wants proof of root cause and a targeted fix.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- vercel:bootstrap: Bootstrap a repository with Vercel-linked resources by running preflight checks, provisioning integrations, verifying env keys, and then executing db/dev startup commands safely.

- vercel:env: Manage Vercel environment variables. Commands include list, pull, add, remove, and diff. Use to sync environment variables between Vercel and your local development environment.
- vercel:marketplace: Discover and install Vercel Marketplace integrations. Use to find databases, CMS, auth providers, and other services available on the Vercel Marketplace.
- vercel:status: Show the status of the current Vercel project — recent deployments, linked project info, and environment overview.

- vercel:auth: Authentication integration guidance — Clerk (native Vercel Marketplace), Descope, and Auth0 setup for Next.js applications. Covers middleware auth patterns, sign-in/sign-up flows, and Marketplace provisioning. Use when implementing user authentication.
- vercel:bootstrap: Project bootstrapping orchestrator for repos that depend on Vercel-linked resources (databases, auth, and managed integrations). Use when setting up or repairing a repository so linking, environment provisioning, env pulls, and first-run db/dev commands happen in the correct safe order.

- vercel:marketplace: Vercel Marketplace expert guidance — discovering, installing, and building integrations, auto-provisioned environment variables, unified billing, and the vercel integration CLI. Use when consuming third-party services, building custom integrations, or managing marketplace resources on Vercel.
- vercel:next-cache-components: Next.js 16 Cache Components guidance — PPR, use cache directive, cacheLife, cacheTag, updateTag, and migration from unstable_cache. Use when implementing partial prerendering, caching strategies, or migrating from older Next.js cache patterns.
- vercel:next-forge: next-forge expert guidance — production-grade Turborepo monorepo SaaS starter by Vercel. Use when working in a next-forge project, scaffolding with `npx next-forge init`, or editing @repo/* workspace packages.
- vercel:next-upgrade: Upgrade Next.js to the latest version following official migration guides and codemods. Use when upgrading Next.js versions, running codemods, or migrating between major releases.

- vercel:react-best-practices: React best-practices reviewer for TSX files. Triggers after editing multiple TSX components to run a condensed quality checklist covering component structure, hooks usage, accessibility, performance, and TypeScript patterns.
- vercel:routing-middleware: Vercel Routing Middleware guidance — request interception before cache, rewrites, redirects, personalization. Works with any framework. Supports Edge, Node.js, and Bun runtimes. Use when intercepting requests at the platform level.
- vercel:runtime-cache: Vercel Runtime Cache API guidance — ephemeral per-region key-value cache with tag-based invalidation. Shared across Functions, Routing Middleware, and Builds. Use when implementing caching strategies beyond framework-level caching.

Use when initializing shadcn, adding components, composing product UI, building custom registries, configuring themes, or troubleshooting component issues.

- vercel:vercel-agent: Vercel Agent guidance — AI-powered code review, incident investigation, and SDK installation. Automates PR analysis and anomaly debugging. Use when configuring or understanding Vercel's AI development tools.
- vercel:vercel-cli: Vercel CLI expert guidance. Use when deploying, managing environment variables, linking projects, viewing logs, querying metrics, managing domains, or interacting with the Vercel platform from the command line.

Use when configuring platform-level security, responding to attacks, or staging firewall rules.
- vercel:vercel-functions: Vercel Functions expert guidance — Serverless Functions, Edge Functions, Fluid Compute, streaming, Cron Jobs, and runtime configuration. Use when configuring, debugging, or optimizing server-side code running on Vercel.
- vercel:vercel-sandbox: Vercel Sandbox guidance — ephemeral Firecracker microVMs for running untrusted code safely. Supports AI agents, code generation, and experimentation. Use when executing user-generated or AI-generated code in isolation.
- vercel:vercel-storage: Vercel storage expert guidance — Blob, Edge Config, and Marketplace storage (Neon Postgres, Upstash Redis). Use when choosing, configuring, or using data storage with Vercel applications.
- vercel:verification: Full-story verification — infers what the user is building, then verifies the complete flow end-to-end: browser → API → data → response. Triggers on dev server start and 'why isn't this working' signals.
- vercel:workflow: Vercel Workflow DevKit (WDK) expert guidance. Use when building durable workflows, long-running tasks, API routes or agents that need pause/resume, retries, step-based execution, or crash-safe orchestration with Vercel Workflow.
- cowork-plugin-management:cowork-plugin-customizer: Customize a Claude Code plugin for a specific organization's tools and workflows. Use when: customize plugin, set up plugin, configure plugin, tailor plugin, adjust plugin settings, customize plugin connectors, customize plugin skill, tweak plugin, modify plugin configuration.
- cowork-plugin-management:create-cowork-plugin: Guide users through creating a new plugin from scratch in a cowork session. Use when users want to create a plugin, build a plugin, make a new plugin, develop a plugin, scaffold a plugin, start a plugin from scratch, or design a plugin. This skill requires Cowork mode with access to the outputs directory for delivering the final .plugin file.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:mcp-builder: Guide for creating high-quality MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers that enable LLMs to interact with external services through well-designed tools. Use when building MCP servers to integrate external APIs or services, whether in Python (FastMCP) or Node/TypeScript (MCP SDK).
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

file.content:
---
name: feedback-branch-pr-flow
description: "Albert's git workflow — sync develop into sandbox-albert before new code, ship via PR to develop, in both repos"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: feedback
  originSessionId: ef9660ab-2c3b-4a4e-b8b3-493d852d262c
---

For both `designflow-frontend` and `designflow-backend` (and likely the other repos): before adding new code to `sandbox-albert`, first pull/merge the latest `origin/develop` into `sandbox-albert`, then write the code on top. Ship the work by opening a PR from `sandbox-albert` → `develop` (use `gh pr create --base develop --head sandbox-albert`). Always include unit tests for new functions (see [[feedback_ag_grid_rules]]).

**Why:** Albert asked for exactly this flow on 2026-05-29 (price_sales_snapshots feature): "pull the latest changes from develop into sandbox-albert, then start adding your code" and "create a PR for sandbox-albert with develop". The repos' own CLAUDE.md and [[project_designflow]] still say "sandbox-albert only, never merge" — when asked, Albert chose to do the develop→sandbox-albert merge but **leave that rule text unchanged**, so the docs intentionally conflict with this practice.

**How to apply:** When starting work, `git fetch origin` then merge `origin/develop` into `sandbox-albert` (confirm first if there are conflicts or uncommitted work). Commit feature + docs, push `sandbox-albert`, open the PR to `develop`. Backend changes accidentally made on the `develop` branch should be moved to `sandbox-albert` (stash → checkout → pop). Do not edit the "never merge" wording in CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md unless Albert says so.

content.content: 1 # Handoff: Item Detail Page Redesign (DesignFlow Item Library) 2 3 ## Overview 4 This is a redesign of the **item detail page** in the DesignFlow item-library app 5 (the screen at a route like `/apps/itemDetail/:id`). The original screen rendered 6 ~40 product fields as flat, ungrouped read-only rows split across two unlabeled 7 sub-tabs, with a utilitarian Material look. This redesign turns it into a polished, 8 **data-dense but elegant ERP** screen: a clear identity header, a production-timeline 9 band, a product image gallery, grouped + color-coded attribute fields, and supporting 10 panels (product team, tech pack/files, tags, comments), plus fully designed 11 **Licensing** and **Calendar** tabs. 12 13 The design is delivered as **three layout directions** (A, B, C). **Direction A is the 14 selected/approved one** — build that first. B and C are included as alternates and to 15 show the same system applied to different layouts. 16 17 --- 18 19 ## Screenshots 20 Full renders of every board live in `screenshots/` and are embedded inline in the 21 **Screens / Views** section below. Quick index: 22 23 | Board | File | 24 |---|---| 25 | A · Main (selected) | `screenshots/a-main.png` | 26 | A · Licensing | `screenshots/a-licensing.png` | 27 | A · Calendar | `screenshots/a-calendar.png` | 28 | B · Sidebar workspace | `screenshots/b-sidebar-workspace.png` | 29 | C · Brand band + bento | `screenshots/c-brand-band-bento.png` | 30 31 ![A · Main](screenshots/a-main.png) 32 33 --- 34 35 ## About the Design Files 36 The files in `prototype/` are **design references created in HTML/React+Babel** — they 37 are runnable prototypes that show the intended look, layout, and behavior. **They are 38 not production code to copy verbatim.** They use in-browser Babel, global `window` 39 exports, and inline-style objects purely so they run as a standalone artifact. 40 41 Your task is to **recreate these designs in the target codebase's existing 42 environment**, using its established patterns, component library, and styling approach: 43 44 - The original app is **Angular + Angular Material** (the `itemDetail` component lives 45 at `src/app/pages/itemDetail/`). If you are implementing inside that app, build these 46 as Angular components/templates with the app's existing Material theme, `fxLayout`/CSS 47 grid, and data bindings. 48 - If implementing in a different stack (React/Vue/etc.), use that stack's idioms. 49 - Treat the inline styles in the prototype as a **spec of exact values**, not as the 50 way to ship styling. Move them into the codebase's styling system (SCSS, CSS modules, 51 styled-components, Tailwind, Material theme tokens, etc.). 52 53 The mock data in `item-data.js` is invented sample content (a fictional licensed plush 54 product) so nothing real is reproduced. Wire the real fields to the app's actual item 55 model — see **Data Model Mapping** below. 56 57 --- 58 59 ## Fidelity 60 **High-fidelity (hifi).** Colors, typography, spacing, radii, and interactions are all 61 final and intentional. Recreate the UI pixel-accurately using the codebase's libraries, 62 then swap the sample data for live data. Exact values are in **Design Tokens** below and 63 visible inline in the prototype files. 64 65 --- 66 67 ## How to Run the Prototype 68 Open `prototype/Item Detail — Redesign.html` in a browser (or serve the `prototype/` 69 folder). It renders a pan/zoom **design canvas** with all boards: 70 - Section **"Variation A — Two-column dossier"**: three boards — **A · Main**, 71 **A · Licensing**, **A · Calendar** (each board is the same page locked to one tab). 72 - Section **"Other directions"**: **B · Sidebar workspace** and **C · Brand band + bento**. 73 74 The canvas (`design-canvas.jsx`) is presentation scaffolding only — **do not port it.** 75 Only port the page content (the `VariationX` components + the shared atoms). 76 77 File map: 78 | File | What it contains | 79 |---|---| 80 | `item-data.js` | Sample item data + derived timeline/licensing/calendar data (`window.ITEM`). | 81 | `df-components.jsx` | Shared UI atoms (the design system): `Icon`, `IconBadge`, `Pill`, `Field`, `Card`, `Btn`, `Avatar`, `Gallery`, `Timeline`, `TabBar`, `Segmented`, `Row`, `TeamList`, `Comments`, `FileRow`, `AppBar`, etc. | 82 | `variation-a.jsx` | **Selected design.** The Main tab + tab switching. | 83 | `va-tabs.jsx` | Variation A's **Licensing** and **Calendar** tab content + `SummaryBand`, `StageTracker`, `FeedbackTable`, `MiniCalendar`. | 84 | `variation-b.jsx` | Alternate B (sidebar workspace). | 85 | `variation-c.jsx` | Alternate C (brand band + bento). | 86 | `design-canvas.jsx` | Canvas wrapper — presentation only, do not port. | 87 88 --- 89 90 ## Design Tokens 91 92 ### Color — CSS custom properties (defined in the HTML `:root`) 93 ```css 94 --sans: 'Hanken Grotesk', system-ui, -apple-system, sans-serif; 95 --mono: 'IBM Plex Mono', ui-monospace, 'SFMono-Regular', monospace; 96 97 /* Ink / text */ 98 --ink: #1c1b22; /* primary text */ 99 --ink-2: #46454f; /* secondary text */ 100 --ink-3: #76747f; /* tertiary / labels */ 101 --ink-4: #a8a6af; /* muted / empty "—" */ 102 103 /* Lines & surfaces */ 104 --line: #e7e3dd; /* card borders, dividers */ 105 --line-2: #f0ece6; /* lighter inner dividers */ 106 --paper: #ffffff; /* card background */ 107 --bg: #f5f3ef; /* page background (warm neutral) */ 108 --chip: #f1eee9; /* chip / track background */ 109 110 /* Brand (deep indigo) */ 111 --brand: #3f3d86; 112 --brand-700: #302e63; 113 --brand-50: #ecebf6; 114 115 /* Accent (warm clay) — used for "current/due" emphasis */ 116 --accent: #c2662f; 117 --accent-700: #9a4f23; 118 --accent-50: #f7ece2; 119 ``` 120 121 ### Multi-hue icon/category palette (`ICON_TONES` in `df-components.jsx`) 122 Each tone = `{ bg, fg }`. `bg` tints badge/chip backgrounds; `fg` colors the icon, label, and left bar. 123 | Tone | bg | fg | Used for | 124 |---|---|---|---| 125 | indigo | `#eceaf7` | `#403d8c` | Main tab, Item Attributes, Vendor/Note | 126 | violet | `#f1e7f8` | `#7d3ca3` | Licensing tab, Tech Pack, product-type fields | 127 | teal | `#dff0ed` | `#1d7a6d` | Calendar tab, Tags, dimensions, content | 128 | green | `#e6f1e9` | `#2e7a4d` | Comments, Feedback, status/cost | 129 | amber | `#f8eed9` | `#9a6810` | At a glance, Royalty, season/age/lead-time | 130 | clay | `#f8eadf` | `#9c4f22` | Production timeline, Licensor, packaging | 131 | blue | `#e4edfc` | `#285aac` | Product Team, Submissions, UOM/style-guide | 132 | rose | `#fae6ec` | `#aa3957` | Property, Artist | 133 134 ### Status pill tones (`PILL_TONES`) 135 | Tone | bg | fg | dot | 136 |---|---|---|---| 137 | neutral | `--chip` | `--ink-2` | `--ink-3` | 138 | brand | `--brand-50` | `--brand-700` | `--brand` | 139 | success | `#e8f1ea` | `#2f6a45` | `#3f8a5b` | 140 | warning | `#f7efe0` | `#8a6312` | `#c08a2a` | 141 | danger | `#f7e7e4` | `#9a3a30` | `#bd5246` | 142 | accent | `--accent-50` | `--accent-700` | `--accent` | 143 144 ### Typography 145 - **UI font:** Hanken Grotesk (Google Fonts), weights 400/500/600/700/800. 146 - **Mono font:** IBM Plex Mono (Google Fonts), weights 400/500/600 — used for all 147 **codes, SKUs, dates, and numeric figures** (tabular). Enable `font-feature-settings: "tnum" 1`. 148 - Scale (px / weight / line-height): 149 - Page title `h1`: 24 / 700 / 1.12, letter-spacing −0.02em 150 - Card title `h3`: 13.5 / 600 / ~1.2, letter-spacing −0.01em 151 - Card kicker: 10 / 700 / uppercase, letter-spacing 0.09em 152 - Section label: 11 / 700 / uppercase, letter-spacing 0.08em 153 - Field label: 10.5 / 600 / uppercase, letter-spacing 0.07em, colored per category 154 - Field value: 13.5 / 500 / 1.35, color `--ink` 155 - Definition-list row label (B): 12 / 500; value 13 / 500 156 - Body / comment text: 12.5 / 1.5 157 - Mono code chip: 9 / 600; mono inline values: 11–13 / 600 158 - Tab label: 13 / 700 active, 500 inactive 159 - Button: 12.5 / 600 (sm), 12.5 / 600 (md) 160 161 ### Spacing, radius, shadow 162 - **Radii:** cards 12px; inner controls/buttons 8px; field/file icon badge 7px; 163 pills 999px; tags 7px; segmented control 9px (thumb 6px); image hero 14px, thumb 9px. 164 - **Card padding:** header `11px 16px`; body default 16–18px. 165 - **Grid gaps:** page sections 16px; field grid 20px; sidebar stack 16–20px. 166 - **Card shadow:** `0 1px 2px rgba(28,27,34,.04)`. Floating card (C): `0 8px 28px rgba(28,27,34,.10)`. 167 - **Page horizontal padding:** 26px. **App bar height:** 52px. 168 - **Design width:** boards are authored at **1340px** wide. The page is fluid — treat 169 1340 as the reference desktop width; columns are grid/flex and reflow. 170 - **Min hit target:** interactive controls ≥ 28–34px tall. 171 172 --- 173 174 ## Shared Components (the design system — `df-components.jsx`) 175 176 - **`AppBar`** — 52px top chrome: logo tile (brand bg, `layers` icon) + "DesignFlow" 177 wordmark, breadcrumb (`Item Library › EH-18635`), search/bell icons, user avatar. 178 - **`IconBadge({name, tone, size, icon})`** — rounded-square tinted icon chip. The core 179 color primitive: used on card headers, tabs, section labels. 180 - **`Pill({tone, dot, children})`** — 22px status pill, optional leading dot. 181 - **`Field({label, value, hint, mono, strong, accent, wide})`** — uppercase label + 182 value. In A/C the attribute grid uses a **colored variant**: 2.5px colored left bar, 183 colored label, and a tinted mono **MG-code chip** for the `hint` (e.g. `MG01`). 184 - **`FieldGrid({cols, gap})`** — CSS grid, `repeat(cols, minmax(0,1fr))`. 185 - **`Card({title, kicker, action, icon, tone, pad})`** — section container. Header = 186 `IconBadge` (colored by `tone`) + optional kicker + title, right-aligned `action`. 187 - **`SectionLabel({children, hint, icon, tone})`** — uppercase divider label with a 188 small colored badge and a trailing rule. 189 - **`Btn({variant, icon, size})`** — variants: `primary` (brand), `accent` (clay), 190 `solid` (ink), `outline`, `ghost`. Sizes sm (28px) / md (34px). 191 - **`Avatar({initials, idx, size})`** — initials avatar; `idx` cycles 5 tones 192 (`#3b3a7c, #9a5b2e, #2f6a52, #7a3a6a, #2e5c8a`). 193 - **`Gallery({images, layout, heroH})`** — product hero + thumbnail strip. `layout="side"` 194 = vertical thumbs left of hero; `layout="below"` = thumbs under hero. Clicking a thumb 195 selects it as the hero (local state). Images are **placeholders** (captioned drop 196 zones) — wire to real product photos. 197 - **`Timeline({items, compact})`** — horizontal milestone stepper. Item state = 198 `done` (brand, check) / `current` (accent, ring) / `todo` (outline). `compact` hides dates. 199 - **`TabBar({tabs, active, onChange})`** — the **colored primary tabs**. Each tab has a 200 tinted `IconBadge`; active tab gets the tone's `bg` fill, `fg` text, matching underline, 201 and a subtle ring on its badge. Tone map: **Main→indigo (grid), Licensing→violet 202 (layers), Calendar→teal (cal)**. 203 - **`Segmented({options, value, onChange})`** — pill segmented control (First/Second). 204 - **`Row({label, value, hint, mono, tone, last})`** — dense definition-list row (B); 205 with `tone` it gets a colored left bar + colored label + tinted MG chip. 206 - **`TeamList`**, **`Comments`**, **`FileRow`** — list renderers for team, comment 207 threads, and file rows (file kind badge color: PDF `#bd5246`, AI `#c08a2a`, IMG `#3f8a5b`). 208 - **Icons** — `Icon({name, size, sw, color})` is a small inline-SVG line-icon set 209 (stroke 1.7, round caps). Names used: download, upload, edit, plus, check, chevR, 210 chevL, clock, clip, chat, image, tag, ext, user, box, grid, cal, layers, search, 211 bell, star, sparkle, dot. Replace with the codebase's icon library (match line weight). 212 213 --- 214 215 ## Screens / Views 216 217 ### A · Main tab (selected — `variation-a.jsx`, board "A · Main") 218 219 ![A · Main](screenshots/a-main.png) 220 221 **Purpose:** primary view of one item — identity, image, status, key attributes, team, files. 222 **Layout (top → bottom), page padding 26px, vertical gap 16px:** 223 1. **AppBar** (52px). 224 2. **Page header** (`--paper`, bottom border): left = status pills row 225 (`Active` success pill + division brand pill) → `h1` item name → identity row with 226 **Style # / Reference # / Art #** as label+mono pairs. Right = `Open in ColdLion` 227 (outline) + `Update Item Details` (primary) buttons. 228 3. **Primary `TabBar`** (Main / Licensing / Calendar) — colored, full-width, in a 229 `--paper` strip. 230 4. **Production timeline band** — slim full-width card: left = clay `clock` IconBadge + 231 "PRODUCTION / Timeline", center = `Timeline` (Created → Test Report → Photography → 232 Due(current) → On Shelf), right = accent "Due Feb 28" pill. 233 5. **Gallery + At a glance** — CSS grid `1fr 340px`: 234 - Left card: `Gallery layout="side"` (hero ~306px tall + vertical thumbs). 235 - Right card "At a glance" (amber): Licensing(success pill) + Production(warning pill) 236 rows, divider, then a 2-col mini field grid (Season, For Cust, Product Mgr, 237 Std Cost mono, Vendor wide). 238 6. **Item Attributes card** — header row: indigo `grid` badge + "Item Attributes" on the 239 left; `Segmented` First/Second + "N fields · read-only" on the right. Body = **4-column 240 colored field grid** (see Item Attributes spec below). 241 7. **Bottom row** — grid `1fr 1fr 1fr`: **Product Team** (blue, `TeamList`), 242 **Tech Pack & Files** (violet — Upload/Download buttons + `FileRow` list), 243 and a stacked column of **Tags** (teal) + **Comments** (green). 244 245 **Item Attributes (the colored field grid):** 4 columns, gap 20px. Each cell: 246 `borderLeft: 2.5px solid ; paddingLeft: 11px`; label uppercase in ``; 247 optional MG-code chip = mono 9px on `` pill; value 13.5/500 in `--ink` 248 (`—` in `--ink-4` when empty). **First** sub-tab fields & tones: 249 Active Status(green), Item Description(indigo), Product Type·MG01(violet), 250 Property·MG06(rose), Product Sub-Type·MG02(violet), Style Guide·MG07(blue), 251 Product Sub-Sub-Type·MG03(violet), Item Content(teal), Size·MG04(teal), 252 Brand Assurance(green), Season(amber), Product Manager(blue), Licensor·MG05(clay), 253 For Customer·MG15(clay). **Second** sub-tab: Discontinued(green), Item Width(teal), 254 Item Depth(teal), Item Weight(teal), Measure UOM(blue), Weight UOM(blue), 255 Art Source·MG08(violet), Artist·MG09(rose), Age Group·MG10(amber), Packaging(clay), 256 Standard Cost(green), Mfg Lead Time(amber), Vendor(indigo), Item Note(indigo). 257 258 ### A · Licensing tab (`va-tabs.jsx` → `LicensingTab`) 259 260 ![A · Licensing](screenshots/a-licensing.png) 261 262 **Purpose:** track the licensor-approval lifecycle for the item. 263 **Layout (gap 16px):** 264 1. **`SummaryBand`** (violet `layers`): facts = Licensor, Property, Style Guide, Royalty, 265 Contract #; right = status success pill + `Progress` meter (64%). 266 2. **Licensing Timeline card** (violet) → **`StageTracker`**: 5 horizontal stage cards 267 connected by chevrons — **Concept(done) → Sample(done) → Development(current, accent 268 highlighted) → Pre-Production(todo) → Production(todo)**. Each card: numbered/checked 269 token, stage name, status pill, and inner step rows (`label … mono date`). 270 3. **Grid `1fr 332px`:** 271 - **Feedback & Approvals card** (green) → **`FeedbackTable`**: columns 272 `Date · Stage · POP Comment · Status · Assignee` (grid `84px 104px 1fr 108px 150px`). 273 Status pills: Approved→success, Revision→warning, Submitted→brand. Assignee = avatar 274 + name + from(Licensor/POP). 7 rows. 275 - Right column stack: **Royalty & Contract** (amber, field grid), **Submissions** 276 (blue, file list with size·date), **Licensing Contacts** (teal, avatar + role/org + 277 mono email). 278 279 ### A · Calendar tab (`va-tabs.jsx` → `CalendarTab`) 280 281 ![A · Calendar](screenshots/a-calendar.png) 282 283 **Purpose:** see the item's key dates on a month calendar + agenda. 284 **Layout (gap 16px):** 285 1. **`SummaryBand`** (teal `cal`): facts = Created, Due, On Shelf, Lead Time, Season; 286 right = legend (Done=brand, Due=accent, Ship=green dots). 287 2. **Grid `1fr 360px`:** 288 - **`MiniCalendar`** card: month grid (Sun–Sat), default **February 2025**, ◀/▶ buttons 289 navigate Dec 2024 → Mar 2025. Day cells are 74px min-height; a cell with an event gets 290 the event tone's tinted bg, bold colored day number, and a labeled dot. Events plotted 291 from `ITEM.calendar.events`. 292 - **All milestones card** (indigo): agenda list — each row = date badge (mon + mono day) 293 + colored dot + label + status pill (Done/Due/Ship). 294 295 ### B · Sidebar workspace (alternate — `variation-b.jsx`) 296 297 ![B · Sidebar workspace](screenshots/b-sidebar-workspace.png) 298 299 Two columns: **344px sticky sidebar** (`--paper`) + fluid workspace. 300 - Sidebar: `Gallery layout="below"` (hero 252px), item name + description, status pills, 301 divider, **Product Team** (blue section label) `TeamList`, **Tags** (teal) `Tags`. 302 - Workspace: sticky header (Licensing status + Due + Tech Pack/Update buttons), a slim 303 **`Timeline` compact** stepper, the colored `TabBar`, then a **Segmented** First/Second 304 and dense **two-column definition lists** via `Row` — sections colored by hue: 305 First = **Identification**(indigo) + **Merchandising**(violet); 306 Second = **Specifications**(green) + **Sourcing & Dates**(amber). Rows carry the section 307 tone (colored left bar, label, MG chip). Ends with an **Item Note** clay callout. 308 309 ### C · Brand band + bento (alternate — `variation-c.jsx`) 310 311 ![C · Brand band + bento](screenshots/c-brand-band-bento.png) 312 313 - **Indigo gradient brand band** (`linear-gradient(120deg, --brand-700, --brand 62%, #4a48a0)`, 314 white text): status chips, large `h1`, translucent identity chips, white/ghost actions. 315 - A **floating milestone card** overlapping the band (`marginTop:-44`, big shadow) with the 316 clay timeline. 317 - A **bento mosaic** grid `1.35fr 1fr 1fr`: tall Gallery card (spans 2 rows) + At a glance 318 (amber) + Tech Pack (violet) + Product Team (blue) + Tags (teal). 319 - Full-width **Item Attributes** card (colored 4-col grid, same tones as A) + **Comments** 320 (green) card. 321 322 --- 323 324 ## Interactions & Behavior 325 - **Primary tabs** (Main/Licensing/Calendar): clicking switches the active tab 326 (`TabBar onChange`). In the prototype canvas each board is *locked* to one tab for 327 side-by-side review (``); in the real app, render **one 328 page with switchable tabs** (omit `lock`, drive `active` from local state or the route). 329 - **First/Second `Segmented`**: toggles the attribute field set (no fetch; same record). 330 - **Gallery thumbnails**: click selects the hero image (local state). Hero/thumbs are drop 331 zones in the mock — replace with real ``/lazy images. 332 - **Calendar ◀/▶**: steps through Dec 2024–Mar 2025; buttons disable at the ends. 333 - **Buttons** (`Open in ColdLion`, `Update Item Details`, `Upload`/`Download`, `Add 334 feedback`, `Add note`, `Edit`, `View all`): wire to the app's existing actions/routes. 335 "Update Item Details" should open the existing edit flow/dialog. These are presentational 336 in the mock. 337 - **Hover/focus:** add the codebase's standard hover treatment to buttons, tabs, file rows, 338 and thumbnails (prototype keeps them subtle: buttons transition bg/border/color 0.15s). 339 - **Reduced motion / print:** there are no entrance animations to gate; content is the base 340 state, so print/PDF render fine. 341 - **Responsive:** authored at 1340px. Collapse the multi-column grids to fewer columns on 342 narrow widths (e.g. field grid 4→2→1; bottom row 3→1; B sidebar stacks above workspace; 343 C bento to a single column). Keep min field-label legibility. 344 345 ## State Management 346 - `activeTab`: 0 Main / 1 Licensing / 2 Calendar (or route param). 347 - `attrSet`: 0 First / 1 Second (attribute group toggle). 348 - `heroIndex`: selected gallery image. 349 - `calMonthIndex`: 0–3 across Dec 2024…Mar 2025. 350 - **Data fetching:** load the item by id; the Licensing and Calendar tabs read from the 351 same item plus its licensing-tracking and key-date records. In the original Angular app 352 these correspond to the `itemDetail` component data and the 353 `license-tracking` / licensing-stage-tracker component. 354 355 --- 356 357 ## Data Model Mapping 358 `item-data.js` (`window.ITEM`) is **sample data**. Map to the real item record: 359 - **Identity:** `itemNum`(Style #), `refNum`, `artNum`, `displayName`, `itemDescr`, `division`. 360 - **Status:** `activeStatus`, `licensingStatus`, `productionStatus`, `brandAssurance`, `discontinued`. 361 - **Merch groups (the MG codes shown as chips):** `productType`(MG01), `productSubType`(MG02), 362 `productSubSubType`(MG03), `size`(MG04), `licensor`(MG05), `property`(MG06), 363 `styleGuide`(MG07), `artSource`(MG08), `artist`(MG09), `ageGroup`(MG10), `forCustomer`(MG15). 364 *(Confirm exact MG-number→field mapping against the real schema; chips are illustrative.)* 365 - **Specs:** `itemContent`, `itemWidth/Depth/Weight`, `measureUom`, `weightUom`, `packaging`, 366 `standardCost`, `mfgLeadTime`, `vendor`/`vendorNick`, `itemNote`, `season`, `productManager`. 367 - **Dates → `timeline` / `calendar.events`:** `created`, `ftySentTesting`, `testReport`, 368 `ftySentPhoto`, `photography`, `priceTickets`, `dueDate`, `onShelf`. 369 - **`team`** (role/name/initials), **`techPack`** + **`attachments`**, **`tags`**, **`comments`**. 370 - **`licensing`:** `progress`, `status`, `royalty`, `contract`, `contractEnds`, `stages[]` 371 (phase/status/state/steps), `feedback[]`, `submissions[]`, `contacts[]`. 372 373 --- 374 375 ## Assets 376 - **Fonts:** Hanken Grotesk + IBM Plex Mono (Google Fonts). Self-host or use the codebase's 377 font pipeline; keep the mono font for codes/dates/figures. 378 - **Icons:** inline line-SVGs in `df-components.jsx` (`Icon`). Replace with the codebase's 379 icon set; match ~1.7px stroke, round caps. Names listed under Shared Components. 380 - **Product images:** none shipped — `Gallery` renders captioned placeholder drop zones. 381 Wire to the item's real photography (main + detail + packaging shots). 382 - **No raster/logo assets** are required; the DesignFlow wordmark is text + an icon tile. 383 384 --- 385 386 ## Files (in this project) 387 Design reference files bundled under `prototype/`: 388 - `Item Detail — Redesign.html` — entry point (loads all scripts into the canvas). 389 - `item-data.js`, `df-components.jsx`, `variation-a.jsx`, `va-tabs.jsx`, 390 `variation-b.jsx`, `variation-c.jsx`, `design-canvas.jsx` (canvas = do not port). 391 392 ## Suggested Build Order 393 1. Port **Design Tokens** (colors, fonts, spacing) into the codebase's theme. 394 2. Build the shared atoms (`IconBadge`, `Pill`, `Card`, `Field`/colored field, 395 `TabBar`, `Btn`, `Avatar`, `Gallery`, `Timeline`). 396 3. Build **A · Main**, wired to real data, with working tabs + First/Second toggle. 397 4. Build **A · Licensing** (`StageTracker`, `FeedbackTable`) and **A · Calendar** 398 (`MiniCalendar`, agenda). 399 5. Add responsive collapse + hover/focus states from the design system. 400 6. (Optional) B and C if those directions are wanted. 401

file.content: # Handoff: Item Detail Page Redesign (DesignFlow Item Library) ## Overview This is a redesign of the **item detail page** in the DesignFlow item-library app (the screen at a route like `/apps/itemDetail/:id`). The original screen rendered ~40 product fields as flat, ungrouped read-only rows split across two unlabeled sub-tabs, with a utilitarian Material look. This redesign turns it into a polished, **data-dense but elegant ERP** screen: a clear identity header, a production-timeline band, a product image gallery, grouped + color-coded attribute fields, and supporting panels (product team, tech pack/files, tags, comments), plus fully designed **Licensing** and **Calendar** tabs. The design is delivered as **three layout directions** (A, B, C). **Direction A is the selected/approved one** — build that first. B and C are included as alternates and to show the same system applied to different layouts. --- ## Screenshots Full renders of every board live in `screenshots/` and are embedded inline in the **Screens / Views** section below. Quick index: | Board | File | |---|---| | A · Main (selected) | `screenshots/a-main.png` | | A · Licensing | `screenshots/a-licensing.png` | | A · Calendar | `screenshots/a-calendar.png` | | B · Sidebar workspace | `screenshots/b-sidebar-workspace.png` | | C · Brand band + bento | `screenshots/c-brand-band-bento.png` | ![A · Main](screenshots/a-main.png) --- ## About the Design Files The files in `prototype/` are **design references created in HTML/React+Babel** — they are runnable prototypes that show the intended look, layout, and behavior. **They are not production code to copy verbatim.** They use in-browser Babel, global `window` exports, and inline-style objects purely so they run as a standalone artifact. Your task is to **recreate these designs in the target codebase's existing environment**, using its established patterns, component library, and styling approach: - The original app is **Angular + Angular Material** (the `itemDetail` component lives at `src/app/pages/itemDetail/`). If you are implementing inside that app, build these as Angular components/templates with the app's existing Material theme, `fxLayout`/CSS grid, and data bindings. - If implementing in a different stack (React/Vue/etc.), use that stack's idioms. - Treat the inline styles in the prototype as a **spec of exact values**, not as the way to ship styling. Move them into the codebase's styling system (SCSS, CSS modules, styled-components, Tailwind, Material theme tokens, etc.). The mock data in `item-data.js` is invented sample content (a fictional licensed plush product) so nothing real is reproduced. Wire the real fields to the app's actual item model — see **Data Model Mapping** below. --- ## Fidelity **High-fidelity (hifi).** Colors, typography, spacing, radii, and interactions are all final and intentional. Recreate the UI pixel-accurately using the codebase's libraries, then swap the sample data for live data. Exact values are in **Design Tokens** below and visible inline in the prototype files. --- ## How to Run the Prototype Open `prototype/Item Detail — Redesign.html` in a browser (or serve the `prototype/` folder). It renders a pan/zoom **design canvas** with all boards: - Section **"Variation A — Two-column dossier"**: three boards — **A · Main**, **A · Licensing**, **A · Calendar** (each board is the same page locked to one tab). - Section **"Other directions"**: **B · Sidebar workspace** and **C · Brand band + bento**. The canvas (`design-canvas.jsx`) is presentation scaffolding only — **do not port it.** Only port the page content (the `VariationX` components + the shared atoms). File map: | File | What it contains | |---|---| | `item-data.js` | Sample item data + derived timeline/licensing/calendar data (`window.ITEM`). | | `df-components.jsx` | Shared UI atoms (the design system): `Icon`, `IconBadge`, `Pill`, `Field`, `Card`, `Btn`, `Avatar`, `Gallery`, `Timeline`, `TabBar`, `Segmented`, `Row`, `TeamList`, `Comments`, `FileRow`, `AppBar`, etc. | | `variation-a.jsx` | **Selected design.** The Main tab + tab switching. | | `va-tabs.jsx` | Variation A's **Licensing** and **Calendar** tab content + `SummaryBand`, `StageTracker`, `FeedbackTable`, `MiniCalendar`. | | `variation-b.jsx` | Alternate B (sidebar workspace). | | `variation-c.jsx` | Alternate C (brand band + bento). | | `design-canvas.jsx` | Canvas wrapper — presentation only, do not port. | --- ## Design Tokens ### Color — CSS custom properties (defined in the HTML `:root`) ```css --sans: 'Hanken Grotesk', system-ui, -apple-system, sans-serif; --mono: 'IBM Plex Mono', ui-monospace, 'SFMono-Regular', monospace; /* Ink / text */ --ink: #1c1b22; /* primary text */ --ink-2: #46454f; /* secondary text */ --ink-3: #76747f; /* tertiary / labels */ --ink-4: #a8a6af; /* muted / empty "—" */ /* Lines & surfaces */ --line: #e7e3dd; /* card borders, dividers */ --line-2: #f0ece6; /* lighter inner dividers */ --paper: #ffffff; /* card background */ --bg: #f5f3ef; /* page background (warm neutral) */ --chip: #f1eee9; /* chip / track background */ /* Brand (deep indigo) */ --brand: #3f3d86; --brand-700: #302e63; --brand-50: #ecebf6; /* Accent (warm clay) — used for "current/due" emphasis */ --accent: #c2662f; --accent-700: #9a4f23; --accent-50: #f7ece2; ``` ### Multi-hue icon/category palette (`ICON_TONES` in `df-components.jsx`) Each tone = `{ bg, fg }`. `bg` tints badge/chip backgrounds; `fg` colors the icon, label, and left bar. | Tone | bg | fg | Used for | |---|---|---|---| | indigo | `#eceaf7` | `#403d8c` | Main tab, Item Attributes, Vendor/Note | | violet | `#f1e7f8` | `#7d3ca3` | Licensing tab, Tech Pack, product-type fields | | teal | `#dff0ed` | `#1d7a6d` | Calendar tab, Tags, dimensions, content | | green | `#e6f1e9` | `#2e7a4d` | Comments, Feedback, status/cost | | amber | `#f8eed9` | `#9a6810` | At a glance, Royalty, season/age/lead-time | | clay | `#f8eadf` | `#9c4f22` | Production timeline, Licensor, packaging | | blue | `#e4edfc` | `#285aac` | Product Team, Submissions, UOM/style-guide | | rose | `#fae6ec` | `#aa3957` | Property, Artist | ### Status pill tones (`PILL_TONES`) | Tone | bg | fg | dot | |---|---|---|---| | neutral | `--chip` | `--ink-2` | `--ink-3` | | brand | `--brand-50` | `--brand-700` | `--brand` | | success | `#e8f1ea` | `#2f6a45` | `#3f8a5b` | | warning | `#f7efe0` | `#8a6312` | `#c08a2a` | | danger | `#f7e7e4` | `#9a3a30` | `#bd5246` | | accent | `--accent-50` | `--accent-700` | `--accent` | ### Typography - **UI font:** Hanken Grotesk (Google Fonts), weights 400/500/600/700/800. - **Mono font:** IBM Plex Mono (Google Fonts), weights 400/500/600 — used for all **codes, SKUs, dates, and numeric figures** (tabular). Enable `font-feature-settings: "tnum" 1`. - Scale (px / weight / line-height): - Page title `h1`: 24 / 700 / 1.12, letter-spacing −0.02em - Card title `h3`: 13.5 / 600 / ~1.2, letter-spacing −0.01em - Card kicker: 10 / 700 / uppercase, letter-spacing 0.09em - Section label: 11 / 700 / uppercase, letter-spacing 0.08em - Field label: 10.5 / 600 / uppercase, letter-spacing 0.07em, colored per category - Field value: 13.5 / 500 / 1.35, color `--ink` - Definition-list row label (B): 12 / 500; value 13 / 500 - Body / comment text: 12.5 / 1.5 - Mono code chip: 9 / 600; mono inline values: 11–13 / 600 - Tab label: 13 / 700 active, 500 inactive - Button: 12.5 / 600 (sm), 12.5 / 600 (md) ### Spacing, radius, shadow - **Radii:** cards 12px; inner controls/buttons 8px; field/file icon badge 7px; pills 999px; tags 7px; segmented control 9px (thumb 6px); image hero 14px, thumb 9px. - **Card padding:** header `11px 16px`; body default 16–18px. - **Grid gaps:** page sections 16px; field grid 20px; sidebar stack 16–20px. - **Card shadow:** `0 1px 2px rgba(28,27,34,.04)`. Floating card (C): `0 8px 28px rgba(28,27,34,.10)`. - **Page horizontal padding:** 26px. **App bar height:** 52px. - **Design width:** boards are authored at **1340px** wide. The page is fluid — treat 1340 as the reference desktop width; columns are grid/flex and reflow. - **Min hit target:** interactive controls ≥ 28–34px tall. --- ## Shared Components (the design system — `df-components.jsx`) - **`AppBar`** — 52px top chrome: logo tile (brand bg, `layers` icon) + "DesignFlow" wordmark, breadcrumb (`Item Library › EH-18635`), search/bell icons, user avatar. - **`IconBadge({name, tone, size, icon})`** — rounded-square tinted icon chip. The core color primitive: used on card headers, tabs, section labels. - **`Pill({tone, dot, children})`** — 22px status pill, optional leading dot. - **`Field({label, value, hint, mono, strong, accent, wide})`** — uppercase label + value. In A/C the attribute grid uses a **colored variant**: 2.5px colored left bar, colored label, and a tinted mono **MG-code chip** for the `hint` (e.g. `MG01`). - **`FieldGrid({cols, gap})`** — CSS grid, `repeat(cols, minmax(0,1fr))`. - **`Card({title, kicker, action, icon, tone, pad})`** — section container. Header = `IconBadge` (colored by `tone`) + optional kicker + title, right-aligned `action`. - **`SectionLabel({children, hint, icon, tone})`** — uppercase divider label with a small colored badge and a trailing rule. - **`Btn({variant, icon, size})`** — variants: `primary` (brand), `accent` (clay), `solid` (ink), `outline`, `ghost`. Sizes sm (28px) / md (34px). - **`Avatar({initials, idx, size})`** — initials avatar; `idx` cycles 5 tones (`#3b3a7c, #9a5b2e, #2f6a52, #7a3a6a, #2e5c8a`). - **`Gallery({images, layout, heroH})`** — product hero + thumbnail strip. `layout="side"` = vertical thumbs left of hero; `layout="below"` = thumbs under hero. Clicking a thumb selects it as the hero (local state). Images are **placeholders** (captioned drop zones) — wire to real product photos. - **`Timeline({items, compact})`** — horizontal milestone stepper. Item state = `done` (brand, check) / `current` (accent, ring) / `todo` (outline). `compact` hides dates. - **`TabBar({tabs, active, onChange})`** — the **colored primary tabs**. Each tab has a tinted `IconBadge`; active tab gets the tone's `bg` fill, `fg` text, matching underline, and a subtle ring on its badge. Tone map: **Main→indigo (grid), Licensing→violet (layers), Calendar→teal (cal)**. - **`Segmented({options, value, onChange})`** — pill segmented control (First/Second). - **`Row({label, value, hint, mono, tone, last})`** — dense definition-list row (B); with `tone` it gets a colored left bar + colored label + tinted MG chip. - **`TeamList`**, **`Comments`**, **`FileRow`** — list renderers for team, comment threads, and file rows (file kind badge color: PDF `#bd5246`, AI `#c08a2a`, IMG `#3f8a5b`). - **Icons** — `Icon({name, size, sw, color})` is a small inline-SVG line-icon set (stroke 1.7, round caps). Names used: download, upload, edit, plus, check, chevR, chevL, clock, clip, chat, image, tag, ext, user, box, grid, cal, layers, search, bell, star, sparkle, dot. Replace with the codebase's icon library (match line weight). --- ## Screens / Views ### A · Main tab (selected — `variation-a.jsx`, board "A · Main") ![A · Main](screenshots/a-main.png) **Purpose:** primary view of one item — identity, image, status, key attributes, team, files. **Layout (top → bottom), page padding 26px, vertical gap 16px:** 1. **AppBar** (52px). 2. **Page header** (`--paper`, bottom border): left = status pills row (`Active` success pill + division brand pill) → `h1` item name → identity row with **Style # / Reference # / Art #** as label+mono pairs. Right = `Open in ColdLion` (outline) + `Update Item Details` (primary) buttons. 3. **Primary `TabBar`** (Main / Licensing / Calendar) — colored, full-width, in a `--paper` strip. 4. **Production timeline band** — slim full-width card: left = clay `clock` IconBadge + "PRODUCTION / Timeline", center = `Timeline` (Created → Test Report → Photography → Due(current) → On Shelf), right = accent "Due Feb 28" pill. 5. **Gallery + At a glance** — CSS grid `1fr 340px`: - Left card: `Gallery layout="side"` (hero ~306px tall + vertical thumbs). - Right card "At a glance" (amber): Licensing(success pill) + Production(warning pill) rows, divider, then a 2-col mini field grid (Season, For Cust, Product Mgr, Std Cost mono, Vendor wide). 6. **Item Attributes card** — header row: indigo `grid` badge + "Item Attributes" on the left; `Segmented` First/Second + "N fields · read-only" on the right. Body = **4-column colored field grid** (see Item Attributes spec below). 7. **Bottom row** — grid `1fr 1fr 1fr`: **Product Team** (blue, `TeamList`), **Tech Pack & Files** (violet — Upload/Download buttons + `FileRow` list), and a stacked column of **Tags** (teal) + **Comments** (green). **Item Attributes (the colored field grid):** 4 columns, gap 20px. Each cell: `borderLeft: 2.5px solid ; paddingLeft: 11px`; label uppercase in ``; optional MG-code chip = mono 9px on `` pill; value 13.5/500 in `--ink` (`—` in `--ink-4` when empty). **First** sub-tab fields & tones: Active Status(green), Item Description(indigo), Product Type·MG01(violet), Property·MG06(rose), Product Sub-Type·MG02(violet), Style Guide·MG07(blue), Product Sub-Sub-Type·MG03(violet), Item Content(teal), Size·MG04(teal), Brand Assurance(green), Season(amber), Product Manager(blue), Licensor·MG05(clay), For Customer·MG15(clay). **Second** sub-tab: Discontinued(green), Item Width(teal), Item Depth(teal), Item Weight(teal), Measure UOM(blue), Weight UOM(blue), Art Source·MG08(violet), Artist·MG09(rose), Age Group·MG10(amber), Packaging(clay), Standard Cost(green), Mfg Lead Time(amber), Vendor(indigo), Item Note(indigo). ### A · Licensing tab (`va-tabs.jsx` → `LicensingTab`) ![A · Licensing](screenshots/a-licensing.png) **Purpose:** track the licensor-approval lifecycle for the item. **Layout (gap 16px):** 1. **`SummaryBand`** (violet `layers`): facts = Licensor, Property, Style Guide, Royalty, Contract #; right = status success pill + `Progress` meter (64%). 2. **Licensing Timeline card** (violet) → **`StageTracker`**: 5 horizontal stage cards connected by chevrons — **Concept(done) → Sample(done) → Development(current, accent highlighted) → Pre-Production(todo) → Production(todo)**. Each card: numbered/checked token, stage name, status pill, and inner step rows (`label … mono date`). 3. **Grid `1fr 332px`:** - **Feedback & Approvals card** (green) → **`FeedbackTable`**: columns `Date · Stage · POP Comment · Status · Assignee` (grid `84px 104px 1fr 108px 150px`). Status pills: Approved→success, Revision→warning, Submitted→brand. Assignee = avatar + name + from(Licensor/POP). 7 rows. - Right column stack: **Royalty & Contract** (amber, field grid), **Submissions** (blue, file list with size·date), **Licensing Contacts** (teal, avatar + role/org + mono email). ### A · Calendar tab (`va-tabs.jsx` → `CalendarTab`) ![A · Calendar](screenshots/a-calendar.png) **Purpose:** see the item's key dates on a month calendar + agenda. **Layout (gap 16px):** 1. **`SummaryBand`** (teal `cal`): facts = Created, Due, On Shelf, Lead Time, Season; right = legend (Done=brand, Due=accent, Ship=green dots). 2. **Grid `1fr 360px`:** - **`MiniCalendar`** card: month grid (Sun–Sat), default **February 2025**, ◀/▶ buttons navigate Dec 2024 → Mar 2025. Day cells are 74px min-height; a cell with an event gets the event tone's tinted bg, bold colored day number, and a labeled dot. Events plotted from `ITEM.calendar.events`. - **All milestones card** (indigo): agenda list — each row = date badge (mon + mono day) + colored dot + label + status pill (Done/Due/Ship). ### B · Sidebar workspace (alternate — `variation-b.jsx`) ![B · Sidebar workspace](screenshots/b-sidebar-workspace.png) Two columns: **344px sticky sidebar** (`--paper`) + fluid workspace. - Sidebar: `Gallery layout="below"` (hero 252px), item name + description, status pills, divider, **Product Team** (blue section label) `TeamList`, **Tags** (teal) `Tags`. - Workspace: sticky header (Licensing status + Due + Tech Pack/Update buttons), a slim **`Timeline` compact** stepper, the colored `TabBar`, then a **Segmented** First/Second and dense **two-column definition lists** via `Row` — sections colored by hue: First = **Identification**(indigo) + **Merchandising**(violet); Second = **Specifications**(green) + **Sourcing & Dates**(amber). Rows carry the section tone (colored left bar, label, MG chip). Ends with an **Item Note** clay callout. ### C · Brand band + bento (alternate — `variation-c.jsx`) ![C · Brand band + bento](screenshots/c-brand-band-bento.png) - **Indigo gradient brand band** (`linear-gradient(120deg, --brand-700, --brand 62%, #4a48a0)`, white text): status chips, large `h1`, translucent identity chips, white/ghost actions. - A **floating milestone card** overlapping the band (`marginTop:-44`, big shadow) with the clay timeline. - A **bento mosaic** grid `1.35fr 1fr 1fr`: tall Gallery card (spans 2 rows) + At a glance (amber) + Tech Pack (violet) + Product Team (blue) + Tags (teal). - Full-width **Item Attributes** card (colored 4-col grid, same tones as A) + **Comments** (green) card. --- ## Interactions & Behavior - **Primary tabs** (Main/Licensing/Calendar): clicking switches the active tab (`TabBar onChange`). In the prototype canvas each board is *locked* to one tab for side-by-side review (``); in the real app, render **one page with switchable tabs** (omit `lock`, drive `active` from local state or the route). - **First/Second `Segmented`**: toggles the attribute field set (no fetch; same record). - **Gallery thumbnails**: click selects the hero image (local state). Hero/thumbs are drop zones in the mock — replace with real ``/lazy images. - **Calendar ◀/▶**: steps through Dec 2024–Mar 2025; buttons disable at the ends. - **Buttons** (`Open in ColdLion`, `Update Item Details`, `Upload`/`Download`, `Add feedback`, `Add note`, `Edit`, `View all`): wire to the app's existing actions/routes. "Update Item Details" should open the existing edit flow/dialog. These are presentational in the mock. - **Hover/focus:** add the codebase's standard hover treatment to buttons, tabs, file rows, and thumbnails (prototype keeps them subtle: buttons transition bg/border/color 0.15s). - **Reduced motion / print:** there are no entrance animations to gate; content is the base state, so print/PDF render fine. - **Responsive:** authored at 1340px. Collapse the multi-column grids to fewer columns on narrow widths (e.g. field grid 4→2→1; bottom row 3→1; B sidebar stacks above workspace; C bento to a single column). Keep min field-label legibility. ## State Management - `activeTab`: 0 Main / 1 Licensing / 2 Calendar (or route param). - `attrSet`: 0 First / 1 Second (attribute group toggle). - `heroIndex`: selected gallery image. - `calMonthIndex`: 0–3 across Dec 2024…Mar 2025. - **Data fetching:** load the item by id; the Licensing and Calendar tabs read from the same item plus its licensing-tracking and key-date records. In the original Angular app these correspond to the `itemDetail` component data and the `license-tracking` / licensing-stage-tracker component. --- ## Data Model Mapping `item-data.js` (`window.ITEM`) is **sample data**. Map to the real item record: - **Identity:** `itemNum`(Style #), `refNum`, `artNum`, `displayName`, `itemDescr`, `division`. - **Status:** `activeStatus`, `licensingStatus`, `productionStatus`, `brandAssurance`, `discontinued`. - **Merch groups (the MG codes shown as chips):** `productType`(MG01), `productSubType`(MG02), `productSubSubType`(MG03), `size`(MG04), `licensor`(MG05), `property`(MG06), `styleGuide`(MG07), `artSource`(MG08), `artist`(MG09), `ageGroup`(MG10), `forCustomer`(MG15). *(Confirm exact MG-number→field mapping against the real schema; chips are illustrative.)* - **Specs:** `itemContent`, `itemWidth/Depth/Weight`, `measureUom`, `weightUom`, `packaging`, `standardCost`, `mfgLeadTime`, `vendor`/`vendorNick`, `itemNote`, `season`, `productManager`. - **Dates → `timeline` / `calendar.events`:** `created`, `ftySentTesting`, `testReport`, `ftySentPhoto`, `photography`, `priceTickets`, `dueDate`, `onShelf`. - **`team`** (role/name/initials), **`techPack`** + **`attachments`**, **`tags`**, **`comments`**. - **`licensing`:** `progress`, `status`, `royalty`, `contract`, `contractEnds`, `stages[]` (phase/status/state/steps), `feedback[]`, `submissions[]`, `contacts[]`. --- ## Assets - **Fonts:** Hanken Grotesk + IBM Plex Mono (Google Fonts). Self-host or use the codebase's font pipeline; keep the mono font for codes/dates/figures. - **Icons:** inline line-SVGs in `df-components.jsx` (`Icon`). Replace with the codebase's icon set; match ~1.7px stroke, round caps. Names listed under Shared Components. - **Product images:** none shipped — `Gallery` renders captioned placeholder drop zones. Wire to the item's real photography (main + detail + packaging shots). - **No raster/logo assets** are required; the DesignFlow wordmark is text + an icon tile. --- ## Files (in this project) Design reference files bundled under `prototype/`: - `Item Detail — Redesign.html` — entry point (loads all scripts into the canvas). - `item-data.js`, `df-components.jsx`, `variation-a.jsx`, `va-tabs.jsx`, `variation-b.jsx`, `variation-c.jsx`, `design-canvas.jsx` (canvas = do not port). ## Suggested Build Order 1. Port **Design Tokens** (colors, fonts, spacing) into the codebase's theme. 2. Build the shared atoms (`IconBadge`, `Pill`, `Card`, `Field`/colored field, `TabBar`, `Btn`, `Avatar`, `Gallery`, `Timeline`). 3. Build **A · Main**, wired to real data, with working tabs + First/Second toggle. 4. Build **A · Licensing** (`StageTracker`, `FeedbackTable`) and **A · Calendar** (`MiniCalendar`, agenda). 5. Add responsive collapse + hover/focus states from the design system. 6. (Optional) B and C if those directions are wanted.

/* Mock item for the DesignFlow item-detail redesign.
   Fields mirror the real itemDetail component data model (merch groups,
   dates, assignments, licensing) but use an invented, non-branded
   licensed property so nothing real is recreated. */
window.ITEM = {
  // Identity
  itemNum: "EH-18635",
  refNum: "RF-22041",
  artNum: "ART-9087",
  displayName: "Pip the Fox · 16\" Shaped Plush Cushion",
  itemDescr: "Shaped plush cushion, embroidered face — Woodland Friends collection",
  division: "POP Lic",
  divisionFull: "POP Licensing — Edgehome",

  // Status
  licensingStatus: "Approved — Final Art",
  licensingStage: "approved", // concept | design | submitted | approved
  productionStatus: "In Sampling",
  activeStatus: "Active",
  discontinued: "No",
  brandAssurance: "Cleared",

  // Merchandising (First tab)
  productType: "Plush & Soft Goods",
  productSubType: "Shaped Cushion",
  productSubSubType: "Embroidered",
  property: "Woodland Friends",
  licensor: "Maple & Co. Licensing",
  styleGuide: "SS25 Core",
  itemContent: "100% Polyester shell · PP cotton fill",
  size: "16 in",
  season: "SS25",
  productManager: "Adam Reyes",
  custDesigned: "No",
  forCustomer: "Hearthside Retail",

  // Specs (Second tab)
  itemWidth: "16 in",
  itemDepth: "6 in",
  itemWeight: "0.42 kg",
  measureUom: "in",
  weightUom: "kg",
  itemNote: "Match Pantone 165C on fox fur. Reinforce nose & ear seams; double-stitch.",
  artSource: "In-house Studio",
  packaging: "Polybag with hangtag",
  artist: "J. Rivera",
  standardCost: "$4.85",
  ageGroup: "3+",
  mfgLeadTime: "75 days",
  vendor: "Sunrise Toys Ltd.",
  vendorNick: "Sunrise (Factory 04)",

  // Tags
  tags: ["woodland", "plush", "fox", "SS25", "cushion", "embroidered", "kids"],

  // Dates / timeline
  created: "Dec 3, 2024",
  dueDate: "Feb 28, 2025",
  onShelf: "Mar 14, 2025",
  testReport: "Jan 22, 2025",
  photography: "Feb 10, 2025",
  priceTickets: "Feb 12, 2025",
  ftySentTesting: "Jan 8, 2025",
  ftySentPhoto: "Jan 30, 2025",
  earliestOnShelf: "Mar 14, 2025",

  // Product team (assignments)
  team: [
    { role: "Creative Designer",  name: "Maya Chen",      initials: "MC" },
    { role: "Creative Director",  name: "Daniel Okafor",  initials: "DO" },
    { role: "Technical Designer", name: "Priya Nair",     initials: "PN" },
    { role: "Licensing",          name: "Sofia Romano",   initials: "SR" },
    { role: "Salesperson",        name: "Adam Reyes",     initials: "AR" },
  ],

  // Tech pack & attachments

  attachments: [
    { name: "Techpack_EH18635_v3.pdf", kind: "PDF", size: "2.4 MB" },

    { name: "Lab_dip_approval.jpg",    kind: "IMG", size: "880 KB" },

  // Comments / notes
